blocks.content:
System Design Interview
Follow-Up Questions & Answers
Compiled from 32 real system design problems. These are the deep-dive questions interviewers ask after your
initial design.
I. Scaling & Performance
Q: What happens when your queries start taking longer as your dataset grows?
Add indexes on frequently queried columns first — an index on email turns a 10M-row scan into a few lookups.
Then consider:
 Denormalize tables to eliminate expensive joins
 Add read replicas to distribute read load
 Add Redis cache for hot data; if all data is accessed evenly, add a read replica instead
 Shard the DB by a logical key if write volume is the bottleneck
■ Example: Yelp: added Elasticsearch alongside PostgreSQL for business search; Kafka syncs changes in <1s
Q: How do you handle millions of concurrent reads for the same cached data (hot key)?
 Request coalescing: combine concurrent misses for the same key into a single backend request
 Key fan-out: store identical copies under N different keys (post123-0..post123-N); clients pick randomly
 Probabilistic early refresh: if TTL is 60min, refresh at 50min to prevent thundering herd
 Redundant caches: replicate hot keys across multiple Redis nodes
■ Example: FB News Feed: celebrity posts replicated across multiple Redis nodes to spread read load
Q: What happens when multiple requests try to rebuild an expired cache entry simultaneously
(thundering herd)?
 Use a distributed lock (Redis SETNX) — only one request rebuilds; others wait and get the result
 Probabilistic early refresh: refresh before expiry so the key never actually expires under load
 Promise/Singleflight pattern: first request fetches from DB; all others wait for that single result
■ Example: Ticketmaster: singleflight on 'Event Details' cache expiry
Q: How do you handle resharding when you need to add more shards?
 Use a fixed set of logical buckets (e.g., 256 slots) mapped to physical shards
 Add new shards, remap some buckets, start dual writes (old + new shard)
 Backfill data using COPY or batched SELECT + INSERT with ON CONFLICT upsert (idempotent)
 Stream ongoing changes via CDC/logical replication to close the gap
 When replication lag hits zero, flip reads to new shard; stop dual writes
 Repeat slot by slot; monitor replication lag and error rates throughout
Q: What happens when you have a hot key that's too popular for even a single shard?
Spread the hot key across multiple sub-keys and sum on reads:
 Dynamic splitting: post123-0..post123-N when a key gets hot
 Readers check all possible sub-keys and aggregate
 Tradeoff: read amplification + coordination overhead, but keeps system stable under spikes
■ Example: YouTube: viral video view counter sharded across multiple Redis keys; summed at read time

Q: How do you ensure Read-Your-Writes consistency with read replicas?
 Pin the user's session to the primary DB for 10 seconds after a write
 Or: route reads to primary if request_time < last_write_time + 10s
 Use 'Session Consistency' — track the replication position and wait for replica to catch up
■ Example: Yelp: after writing a review, user is pinned to primary for 10s so they see their own update
Q: How do you handle global latency for users far from your servers?
 CDN for static/semi-static content (URL mappings, business data, video chunks)
 Geographical sharding: shard data by user region (US-East, EU-West)
 Edge caching: push pre-computed feeds and manifests to CDN edge nodes
 Multi-region deployment: each region has its own DB + services; global routing layer
■ Example: URL Shortener: CDN stores short→long mapping at edge; redirect time drops from 300ms to <50ms
II. Storage & Uploads
Q: What if the upload fails at 99%?
 Use S3 multipart upload — each part is independently acknowledged with an ETag
 On failure: call S3 ListParts(uploadId) to find which parts succeeded
 Re-upload only the missing/failed parts from the last successful chunk
 Store uploadId in localStorage so uploads survive app restarts
 Set S3 lifecycle policy to clean incomplete uploads after 1–2 days (they cost money)
■ Example: Dropbox: client tracks upload session; resumes from exact failure point on reconnect
Q: How do you prevent abuse of file uploads?
 Don't let users immediately access what they upload — use a quarantine bucket
 Run virus scan + content validation + file type check before moving to public bucket
 Image recognition to detect inappropriate content; file type validation (photo ≠ executable)
 Include file size limits in presigned URL conditions — prevents storage bombs
 Processing delay naturally throttles abuse: attackers can't immediately verify if upload worked
Q: How do you ensure downloads are fast?
 Serve everything through CDN with appropriate cache headers
 Enable HTTP range requests for large files — allows resumable downloads
 CDN caches at edge: first user pulls from origin; subsequent users in region get <5ms response
 For extreme cases: parallel chunk downloads (4–6 simultaneous) can 3–4x speed
 Most users are limited by total bandwidth, not per-connection limits — CDN is usually enough
■ Example: YouTube: HLS adaptive bitrate — client fetches appropriate quality chunk per network conditions
III. Real-Time & Messaging
Q: How do you scale WebSocket servers when users can connect to any instance?
 Use consistent hashing to route users to the same server instance
 Store server→user mapping in Zookeeper or Redis

 On new connection: query Zookeeper for correct instance; redirect if needed
 On server failure: new instance takes over; Zookeeper updates mapping; clients reconnect
■ Example: FB Live Comments: Zookeeper stores which instance serves which video; SSE connections routed accordingly
Q: How do you deliver messages to offline users?
 Store messages in a per-user Redis queue (list) with TTL = 30 days
 On reconnect: flush all queued messages to client in order
 For push notifications: APNs (iOS) / FCM (Android) when user is offline
 Track delivery per device ID — user with 5 devices needs 5 acknowledgments
■ Example: WhatsApp: Redis offline queue + APNs/FCM; message deleted from S3 after 30 days if not downloaded
Q: How do you handle group message fan-out at scale?
 Publish group message to Kafka; fan-out service writes to each member's queue asynchronously
 For large groups (>256 members): rate-limit fan-out to prevent queue flooding
 Cap group size to bound fan-out cost (WhatsApp: 1024 members)
 Hybrid: small groups get immediate fan-out; large groups use pull-on-read
■ Example: WhatsApp: Kafka fan-out + per-member Redis queues; group size capped at 1024
Q: How do you handle message ordering in a distributed system?
 Server assigns monotonically increasing sequence numbers per chat (Redis INCR per chat_id)
 Client buffers out-of-order messages and displays in sequence number order
 Use server-side timestamps (not client) to sequence events — prevents clock skew issues
■ Example: WhatsApp: client-generated timestamp + server-assigned sequence number; client reorders on display
IV. Concurrency & Transactions
Q: How do you prevent deadlocks with pessimistic locking?
 Always acquire locks in a consistent deterministic order (e.g., sort by user_id or resource_id)
 If locking users 123 and 456, always lock 123 first regardless of business logic order
 Set transaction timeouts as a fallback — deadlocked transactions get killed and retried
 Most modern DBs have automatic deadlock detection; treat it as fallback, not primary strategy
Q: How do you handle the ABA problem with optimistic concurrency?
Use a column that always changes, not one that might return to a previous value:
 Use review_count (monotonically increasing) instead of avg_rating as the version check
 'Update avg_rating to X, increment count to 101, but only if current count is 100'
 When no naturally changing column exists: add an explicit version column that increments on every update
■ Example: Yelp: review_count used as optimistic lock version; avg_rating alone would cause ABA problem
Q: What if your coordinator service crashes during a distributed transaction (2PC)?
 Coordinator writes transaction state to persistent log before each phase
 On restart: new coordinator reads log, determines in-flight transactions, completes them
 Most enterprise transaction managers handle this automatically
 Prefer Saga pattern for resilience — no locks held across network calls; coordinator failure just pauses
progress

Q: What about performance when everyone wants the same resource?
 First: question whether you can change the problem (10 identical items instead of 1 auction?)
 For truly hot resources: queue-based serialization — single worker processes requests sequentially
 Queue absorbs traffic spikes; worker processes at sustainable rate
 Tradeoff: higher latency, but system stays stable instead of grinding to a halt
■ Example: Online Auction: shard by AuctionID so all bids for one item hit one DB node with local row locks
V. Queue & Job Patterns
Q: What happens if a worker crashes while processing a job?
 Worker sends periodic heartbeats to the queue
 If heartbeat is missed, queue assumes worker is dead and re-queues the job
 Job must be idempotent — safe to process multiple times
 Use idempotency keys to detect and skip duplicate processing
Q: What happens if a job keeps failing (poison pill)?
 After 3–5 failures, move job to a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ)
 DLQ isolates bad jobs; healthy work continues unblocked
 DLQ is a collection for human investigation — fix bug or clean data, then re-queue
 SQS has built-in DLQ support; Kafka: create a dlq topic, produce failed records there with error metadata
Q: How do you prevent duplicate work (user clicks submit 3 times)?
 Require a unique idempotency key per operation (user_id + action + timestamp rounded to window)
 Before starting work: check if key already exists in Redis/DB
 If exists: return existing job ID instead of creating a new one
 For system-generated jobs: use deterministic IDs based on input data
Q: How do you handle queue backpressure on Black Friday (10x traffic)?
 Set queue depth limits; return 503/429 when queue is too deep — reject work you can't handle
 Autoscale workers based on queue depth (not CPU — by the time CPU is high, queue is already backed up)
 CloudWatch alarm on queue depth → Auto Scaling group adds workers
 Shed load gracefully: prioritize critical jobs; defer non-critical ones
Q: How do you handle mixed workloads (5-second jobs vs. 5-hour jobs in same queue)?
 Separate queues by job type or expected duration (fast queue / slow queue)
 Prevents head-of-line blocking — fast jobs don't wait behind slow ones
 If duration is unpredictable: start in fast queue; move to slow queue if it exceeds a time limit
 Break large jobs into smaller chunks that use the same queue infrastructure
Q: How do you orchestrate jobs with dependencies (fetch → generate PDF → email)?
 Simple chains: each worker enqueues the next job before marking itself complete
 Include full context in each job so steps can be retried independently
 Complex workflows: use AWS Step Functions, Temporal, or Airflow
 These tools handle retries per step, branching, parallel steps, and visibility into stuck workflows

Q: How would you implement a virtual waiting room for popular events?
 Kafka preserves arrival order + durability; Redis sorted set gives instant position lookup
 On arrival: create idempotency key (userId+eventId), dedupe with Redis SETNX, produce to Kafka
 Consumer reads Kafka → inserts user into Redis sorted set (score = timestamp)
 Async worker: ZPOPMIN N users every 200–500ms; issue signed short-lived access tokens
 Tune batch size to keep downstream utilization at 70–80%; auto-tune with backpressure signals
 Use SSE to push estimated wait time to users (prefer over polling)
 On failure: Kafka is source of truth; resume from last committed offset; repopulate Redis sorted set
VI. Data Integrity & Evolution
Q: How do you guarantee exactly-once processing?
 Use unique request IDs (UUIDs) — check Redis/DB before processing; skip if already seen
 Flink with watermarking for stream deduplication
 Outbox pattern: write event to DB in same transaction as business data; separate publisher reads outbox
 True exactly-once from a queue alone is nearly impossible — use idempotent consumers instead
Q: How do you use the Outbox Pattern?
 Write business data AND event record to same DB transaction (outbox table)
 Separate CDC/poller reads outbox → publishes to Kafka → marks record as sent
 Guarantees: event is published if and only if DB write succeeded
 Prevents the 'payment charged but order not created' split-brain scenario
■ Example: Price Tracker: price change + notification event written atomically; notification only sent if price saved
Q: How do you perform a zero-downtime schema migration on a 1TB table?
 Use the Expand/Contract pattern:
 1. Add new column (nullable) — no downtime
 2. Dual-write to both old and new columns in application code
 3. Backfill old rows in batches (small transactions to avoid lock contention)
 4. Switch reads to new column
 5. Drop old column after confirming no reads
■ Example: Yelp: used expand/contract for schema changes on 200M review table
Q: How do you handle data drift between PostgreSQL and Elasticsearch?
 Use CDC (Debezium) to stream every DB change into Kafka → ES consumer updates index in <1s
 For large drifts: re-index from DB snapshot using index aliases (swap without downtime)
 Monitor index lag as a metric; alert if it exceeds threshold
Q: How do you handle PII and GDPR 'Right to Erasure'?
 Use Crypto-shredding: encrypt each user's data with a unique per-user key
 To 'delete': delete the encryption key — all data (including backups) becomes unreadable
 Soft deletes: set deleted_at; nullify PII fields (name, email) but keep user_id for financial records
Q: How do you handle late-arriving data in stream processing?
 Use watermarking in Flink: allow a configurable window (e.g., 15 minutes) for late events

 Events arriving after the watermark are either discarded or processed in a late-data side output
 Use event time (not processing time) for windowing to handle out-of-order events
■ Example: Ad Click Aggregator: 15-minute watermark for mobile devices on flaky connections
Q: How do you handle schema evolution in a message queue?
 Use a Schema Registry (Confluent Schema Registry with Avro or Protobuf)
 Producers register schema; consumers validate before deserializing
 Backward compatibility: new fields must have defaults; old consumers can still read new messages
 Forward compatibility: old fields must not be removed; new consumers can read old messages
VII. Fault Tolerance & Reliability
Q: What is your MTTR if the primary database region goes dark?
 Promote a read replica to master and update DNS — target MTTR <30 minutes
 Aurora Global Database: automatic failover in <1 minute
 Multi-region active-active: both regions serve traffic; failover is instant but requires conflict resolution
■ Example: Google News: read replica promotion + DNS update; Aurora Global reduces this to <1 min
Q: How do you handle partial failures (payment down but inventory up)?
 Allow users to browse and add to cart (availability) but disable checkout (degraded mode)
 Circuit breaker: if payment service is slow, stop sending requests; give it room to recover
 Exponential backoff with jitter on retries to prevent thundering herd on recovery
■ Example: Local Delivery: browse + add to cart works; checkout disabled when payment service is down
Q: How do you handle cascading failures?
 Circuit breaker: after N failures, open circuit; stop sending requests for T seconds
 Exponential backoff with jitter: prevents all retrying clients from hitting at the same time
 Bulkhead pattern: isolate failures — separate thread pools per downstream service
 Timeout: never wait indefinitely; set aggressive timeouts on all external calls
Q: If you lose the distributed lock provider, do you fail-safe or fail-open?
 For safety-critical operations (ride matching, seat booking): fail-safe — lock everything
 Better to stop matching than allow two riders assigned to the same driver
 For non-critical operations: fail-open with monitoring and alerting
■ Example: Uber: fail-safe on lock provider loss — stop matching rather than risk double-assignment
Q: How do you perform a regional failover without data loss?
 Use synchronous replication across at least two regions
 Only acknowledge write to user once it's persisted in both regions
 Tradeoff: higher write latency (cross-region round trip) vs. zero data loss
■ Example: Online Auctions: synchronous replication; bid only confirmed when persisted in 2 regions
Q: How do you detect and kill zombie workers?
 Workers send heartbeats to orchestrator every N seconds
 If heartbeat missed for 30 seconds: SIGKILL worker and replace
 Kubernetes liveness probes handle this automatically for containerized workers

■ Example: WhatsApp: SSE/WebSocket servers use liveness probes; 30s missed heartbeat → SIGKILL + replace
VIII. System-Specific Deep Dives
Q: How do you avoid showing Tinder profiles the user has already swiped on?
 Small swipe history: server-side cache of swipe data; filter during feed generation
 Large swipe history: Bloom filter — space-efficient probabilistic structure
 Bloom filter: O(1) lookup regardless of history size; false positives possible (skip unswiped profile), never
false negatives (never show swiped profile)
 Tune false positive rate vs. memory usage based on acceptable miss rate
Q: How do you handle 5 devices for 1 WhatsApp user?
 Status table tracks delivery per device_id
 Message stays in queue until all N devices have acknowledged receipt
 Each device has its own encryption key (Signal Protocol sender keys for groups)
Q: How do you handle year-long price history graphs without fetching millions of rows?
 Use TimescaleDB time buckets: aggregate raw data into daily/weekly summaries
 Store multiple resolutions: raw (last 7 days), hourly (last 30 days), daily (last year)
 Query the appropriate resolution based on the requested time range
■ Example: Price Tracker: TimescaleDB continuous aggregates; query daily buckets for year-long charts
Q: How do you handle GPS spoofing by drivers?
 Check if reported speed is physically possible (>200 km/h is suspicious)
 Compare GPS coordinates against road network (driver shouldn't be in a lake)
 Detect sudden location jumps (teleportation between updates)
 Cross-validate with accelerometer/gyroscope data from device sensors
■ Example: Uber: ML model detects anomalous location patterns; suspicious drivers flagged for review
Q: How do you handle connection pooling?
 Keep a small set of warm DB connections instead of creating one per request
 Opening a connection is expensive (~50ms); pool amortizes this cost
 Configure: max pool size, idle timeout, health check interval
 Cap concurrency to prevent overloading the DB — pool size = DB max_connections / num_app_instances
